result:
I keep thinking about the silence that follows a finished book. You turn the last page, perhaps linger on the acknowledgments out of loyalty, then close the covers. The world you just inhabited—its smells of gunpowder or lilacs, its private jokes, its ache—doesn’t evaporate, yet it no longer has a physical address. It floats, an orphan constellation you carry under your coat. I like to pretend that when I shelve the book, the story keeps humming inside the dark space between pages, like a trapped bee trying pollen on the ink. One night the ink will wear thin, and characters will step through the weakened fibers and raid my apartment for better metaphors. Maybe they’ll berate me for not noticing that the author misspelled their mother’s name, or for dog-earing page 113. I half expect to wake at 3 a.m. to the sound of boots crossing my kitchen tiles and a woman in a threadbare cloak helping herself to my coffee beans, muttering that the siege has been dragging on too long and caffeine rations are critical.

I’ve always suspected that objects accrete silence the way ships accrete barnacles. My grandmother’s mantel clock broke when I was eight, and no one wound it again, yet it stood ticking in dreams for decades, counting down something other than hours. When we sold the house, the estate assessor labeled it “decorative/non-functioning,” which felt like a diagnosis. I imagined the clock filing that verdict away in its brass guts, sighing, “Finally, a proper epitaph.” After the closing, I walked the empty rooms, and every footstep ricocheted like a shout hurled down a well. The silence wasn’t quiet; it was the accumulated hush of birthdays, quarrels, Christmas eve fumblings with batteries that never fit, all compressed into a hum almost too low to register. I pictured that hum settling into the studs, waiting for new drywall to bury it alive.

There’s a city at the back of my skull, built entirely from unfinished conversations. Sidewalks are paved with “I should have said—,” traffic lights blink in stuttered apologies. Some streets dead-end into the words “I love you” that never reached the mouth on time; others circle endlessly around an argument that ended with someone slamming a door in 1997. Periodically I receive postcards from the mayor: “Population booming—please stop abandoning sentences mid-syntax.” The postmarks smear under my thumb, because the ink is wet with guilt. At night neon signs flicker, advertising better comebacks I can’t afford. A river of almost-phrases divides the uptown of restraint from the docks of impulse. I keep meaning to bulldoze the whole place, erect a polite park, but the citizens riot, brandishing the torches of their half-lived lives.

When I travel, I collect obsolete names for weather. Icelanders have “hundslappadrífa,” snowflakes as large as a dog’s paw, drifting almost horizontally. In rural Japan, “kitsune-bi” refers to fox-fire heat lightning that dances over rice paddies on summer evenings, luring travelers off safe paths. I borrow these words the way others pocket hotel shampoo, and smuggle them home to deploy during bland conversations. “Looks like hundslappadrífa out there,” I’ll announce in a Chicago elevator, watching fellow passengers nod nervously, wondering whether to seek shelter. The words taste like foreign currency I can’t spend but refuse to throw away. Sometimes I whisper them aloud when insomnia presses its thumb against my sternum, reminding myself the planet is wider than my ceiling.

I mistrust the phrase “in real life,” as though the rest is counterfeit. As though the breath escaping my lungs when a fictional child dies is somehow budget grief, less legitimate than the tears I shed at an actual funeral. But my body refuses accounting. The adrenal spike when the internet delivers catastrophic headlines feels indistinguishable from the moment in a story when the protagonist steps onto the ice and hears it creak. I have only one biology; it reacts wherever danger is convincingly rendered. Perhaps reality is not a domain but a skill: some moments achieve it, others fail. I’ve stood in parking lots that felt more fictional than any dream, conversely felt more awake inside a symphony than inside several jobs I’ve held. I keep this heretical ledger privately, noting when each hour earns the stamp “real,” the way a bouncer wristbands the lucky few allowed into the club of undeniable existence.

My phone’s predictive text longs to be my ghostwriter. I type “The heart is—” and it volunteers “—a lonely hunter.” I refuse the cliché, but the algorithm persists, offering “—a lonely hunter, still.” The still feels almost like penance, or irony. I wonder how many unfinished poems leak into the cloud, puddling there into a vast communal unconscious of aborted metaphors. Eventually the servers will ferment them into a new scripture, passages like “O my lilac heart of dog-tongued snow.” People will cite the line, debate its provenance. Scholars will insist it references climate grief; twitter wits will quip: “Sounds like someone’s AI got drunk on Dickinson.” None of us will recall that it began as a lonely human pressing backspace repeatedly, while outside real lilacs smelled too overwhelming to bear.

Lately I notice my memories turning into museums during closing hours. The lights dim, motion sensors rest. I walk the halls anyway, because I know the layout. Here is the gallery of my mother’s dresses stitched from 1970s bark-cloth, patterns loud as arguments; over there, the corridor of school corridors, lockers slamming canonically like iron lungs. I reach to touch a painting—sunlight on my tenth birthday cake—only to find it replaced by a placard: “Temporarily on loan elsewhere.” The docent, a younger self who never imagined curatorial duties, shrugs: “We rotate to keep things fresh for returning visitors.” I want to protest that I never left, but she’s already escorting out a troupe of tourists speaking the language of tomorrow. I exit through the gift shop, buy a postcard I immediately lose, proof that I was here, or evidence that I wasn’t.

I fantasize about a library where no book contains the same story twice, opening fresh every time you return. Today Anna Karenina decides to take the train to therapy instead of underneath it; tomorrow Ishmael grows weary of watery metaphor and enlists on Wall Street. The librarians greet visitors with the solemnity of surgeons: “Hope you find the version you can survive.” Children skip directly to the last page to check whether adulthood still ends in heartbreak, then solemnly sign consent forms. I volunteer as an evening shelf-reader, ensuring no narrative spills into its neighbor. The pay is terrible—paid in déjà vu—but the work feels critical: someone must maintain possibility, or at least the illusion that revision is built into the fabric of things.

What keeps me awake isn’t fear of death but fear of running out of adjectives for joy. There are only so many ways to say “I awoke and the morning tasted like tangerines” before language turns to compost and nothing surprises. I worry that every sunset borrows royalties from the previous one, until the sky files for bankruptcy. Then one afternoon I watch my neighbor’s kid discover a beetle on the sidewalk—her entire body ignites with the news, she dances a sermon of incomprehension. She doesn’t have words yet, only sound tracks laid end-to-end: “Aaaah! Uh! Yuh!” The beetle, indifferent, trundles toward grass. I realize vocabulary isn’t finite; it recycles like breath, returning to the lungs changed. She gifts me a syllable tomorrow’s poem will pay forward, and the balance sheet regains a single glowing credit. Enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
